title: Ajay Dev
text:
Astrophysicist | Galaxy evolution | Baryons in dark matter haloes | Radio 21-cm | Optical | X-ray 
I am an astrophysicist studying how baryons populate dark matter haloes across cosmic time. My research combines radio, optical, and X-ray observations to measure the cold gas, stellar, and hot gas content of galaxies and galaxy groups, with the broader aim of understanding where the baryons in the Universe reside.
I recently completed my PhD research at the International Centre for Radio Astronomy Research, University of Western Australia. My work focuses on the connection between galaxies and their host dark matter haloes using large astronomical surveys including GAMA, ALFALFA, ASKAP-DINGO, WALLABY, WAVES, and eROSITA.



Neutral hydrogen in galaxies and galaxy groups
The HI–halo mass relation
Galaxy evolution across environment and cosmic time
Baryon fractions in dark matter haloes
Radio spectral-line stacking
Multi-wavelength surveys of stars, cold gas, and hot gas




My current research aims to extend measurements of the baryonic content of dark matter haloes beyond the local Universe using next-generation radio, optical, and X-ray surveys. This includes measuring the evolution of the HI–halo mass relation and combining neutral hydrogen measurements with stellar and hot gas tracers to build a more complete census of baryons in haloes.

title: Research
text:
My research focuses on the baryonic content of dark matter haloes and its evolution across cosmic time. I use multi-wavelength observations to connect the cold gas, stellar, and hot gas components of galaxies and galaxy groups to their underlying halo populations.


Neutral atomic hydrogen is a key tracer of the cold gas reservoir available for future star formation. A major part of my work is measuring how HI content depends on halo mass, environment, and galaxy type.
Using HI spectral stacking, I measure average HI masses for galaxies and groups that are often too faint to detect individually. This allows the HI–halo mass relation to be constrained over a broad range of halo masses.



A central question in galaxy evolution is how the cosmic baryon budget is distributed among stars, cold gas, hot gas, and diffuse material outside haloes. My work combines empirical scaling relations for stellar mass, HI mass, and X-ray gas mass to estimate the baryonic content of haloes at low redshift.



My future research aims to extend these measurements to intermediate redshifts using surveys such as ASKAP-DINGO, WALLABY, MeerKAT HI surveys, WAVES, DEVILS, and eROSITA.
